text: Well, you could try playing with TPU here. Print TPU without infill? Good morning fyi. :) And also as many pointed out, you have the option to print anything as a mold in your slicer options. Also I can not wait to see if you get the new Bambu Lab H2D printer. I'll most likely be getting the base version (do NOT get the laser version).

author: @brandonlewis2599
text: I've experimented with this, but it's not quite as easy as you think. Especially with FDM prints, the layer texture tends to grab parts and lock them in. You need to carefully design the mold to be able to separate. Mold release is essential, but sometimes fails to release, and then you lose the part and the mold.

author: @0garythebloke0
text:
Hi,  as another comment noted, the use of the 'hole' in Tinkercad is essential for producing any design. I started using Tinkercad when I bought my first 3d printer over 6 years ago,  and whilst I'm not exactly a professional I have never found the need to use any other other 3d software.  Yes, whilst it might be under par compared with other well known sculpting products,  it is instantly accessible to complete novices, and more powerful than it might at first appear. I currently have designed over 350 models of varying complexity without having to spend time learning other programs. 
By the way,  really enjoying your YouTube content.👍

author: @TheEmbeddedHobbyist
text: Will the ribbed walls hamper the objects falling out, not sure the wall would be smooth enough even with a release agent?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: ok so you have seen something you think might be problem - which is. great - but what do you think might be a solution if this is problem?

replies.author: @TheEmbeddedHobbyist
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Normally with making plugs to form moulds, there is a fair bit of painting and sanding to get as smooth a surface as possible. or if this is not possible the other route is silicone moulds where you cut the mould apart to release the object.
The only way i can see to do this with a 3D printer is to use a PVA type filament which you can then dissolve away after to release the objects. this does make it a onetime use mould so you would keep having to reprint the moulds.  And of course this will only work if what you are filling the mould with does not dissolve the PVA it itself.

SO i only see the moulds as one time as there is not an easy way of smoothing the inside after printing. Plus there is always the chance of stringers in the voids as the head jumps the gaps.


I'm finding it hard to find a way around this so back to the viewers to see it the accumulated knowledge of the viewer base can help.

author: @donaldhanson2908
text:
twenty-five years ago we saw the potential of 3-d scans and CNC to generate the shape of sculptures to make the foam supports for shipping sculptures; exactly this "make a hole" concept.
I can see the value of using 3d printing to make the wax models of the sculptures now. 
(Personally, I think there is no substitute for an actual bronze sculpture cast in a lost wax mold. but there are ways to bring the process into the 21st century.)

author: @ToninFightsEntropy
text:
Booleans! :D More stable with curve based modellers like this, than polygons, but I still use this a lot in polys anyway! Another interesting form I've seen of it, closer to this with the history objects, was BSP (Binary Space Partition) Modelling in the Unreal Development Kit 2010, which had an awesome 'prefabs' feature using shapes you could make additive or subtractive, in a custom order. You can really do a lot with that sort of thing! In that scenario, you could make a building by hollowing out a smaller duplicates of the cube for each floor, as subtractive, then subtracting smaller cubes for the doors and windows. I made air vents around a mall with a waterfall using that in the end lol, with a big domed roof full of windows, secret tunnels etc.
Combine this with an Extrude Tool and you can make anything ;)

author: @bobweiram6321
text: It will work as long as the mold material is flexible, such as silicone, where the model can be pulled out without damaging the mold, or destructively removed by melting or burning it away as in the case of lost wax casting. For sand or die casting, the model requires a draft angle on the model so it can be pulled cleanly out of the mold without the sides sticking to the mold material.

replies.author: @stewiex
replies.text: Not silicone because it is not a thermal plastic, but you 'can' use thermal polyurethane (TPU) in various soft durometers. It is also very durable as well which is a bonus.

replies.author: @stewiex
replies.text:
Side note: There are other options as well like 'Soft PLA' and TPU like UV Resin for Resin 3d printers (a different type of printer than Robert is using but still widely available and affordable). 

TPU would be my first choice for almost anything although I'm not sure if it is considered food safe for ice cube trays. IMO for a mass producible product like that, silicone is still probably king.

author: @jerrodlopes186
text:
Having worked extensively with mold-making as a lab assistant in the sculpture department of a college I would like to put a few considerations out there for those interested. A primary consideration in mold-making, aside from the overhangs mentioned in the video, is the material that will be used in casting. It is highly recommended that the mold be the opposite of the cast in terms of ductility. In other words, it's not smart to use a solid ABS or resin mold for solid cast parts. Using a solid mold you should cast silicone or rubber parts. Using a silicone or rubber mold you should cast solid ABS or resin parts. I would very highly recommend at least a moderate bit of research into mold-making before setting out to do this. There will be times when you need a mother mold, two part or multi part molds, considerations for self-vulcanizing materials, safety precautions... Don't let any of this slow you down, though. Once you become knowledgeable about mold-making, it can be very rewarding.

PS Rob, you could easily do a video or two on mold-making. Just an idea for you.

Best of luck.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
Boolean operations in 3D CAD are a super awesome feature. With regards to using them to make models in casting, depending on the method you want to use you may need to add something called "draft" which is a very slight taper to the sides that will allow the casting to more easily be removed from the mold over a period of time.
Search this short video for a quick understanding: What is a Draft Angle? || Engineering Drawings for Castings Course Preview  by THORS eLearning Solutions

author: @lilbullet158
text: In Decorative Plastering (cornices corbels, et cetera), those things you call 'Overhangs' are called 'Undercuts'. The usual method of dealing with them is to make the mould out of soft PVC, because it's flexible. There is a more complicated method called a 'Loose Piece Mould'. The oldest method was to make the moulds out of Gelatine.

Not sure you have tinkered with OpenSCAD, it is quite an amazing piece of kit..

author: @Boblee84
text: Just a heads up Rob, there is a feature in Cura under the "special modes" tab called "Mold", which will take any object that you place on the build plate and invert the print in order to give you a mold for that object. Mind you there are disadvantages to using this unless you add sprues and filler holes to your model before hand. Had I realized that you didn't know this about Tinkercad prior I would have mentioned it to you for sure. That is one of my favorite features about tinkercad, it not only allows you to think inside and outside the box, but it lets you turn the box inside out. Also the "hole" feature is super useful when trying to see through an object when fitting pieces together, instead of trying to maneuver your camera like a contortionist lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @WTF_BBQ
text: You can also click on the checkbox  "Transparent"   at the bottom of the color palette  to see through the objects.   This is very useful when you have multiple objects meshed together so it's easier to see what's going on inside them.   And if you want to "Group" these objects together while retaining their individual colors and transparency,  you can check the "Multicolor"  checkbox.
